Xenothrix mcgregori is an extinct New World monkey endemic to Jamaica, discovered in cave deposits. It had a unique dental formula (2.1.3.2) differing from most platyrrhines, and phylogenetic analyses link it closely to titi monkeys (Callicebus and Cheracebus). It was a slow climber with postcranial features distinct from modern primates. DNA evidence suggests it colonized Jamaica about 11 million years ago, separate from other Greater Antillean monkeys.

key_facts: North Pacific right whale is one of the rarest and most endangered whale species with an estimated population of about 335 individuals split between eastern and western North Pacific. It is a very large baleen whale specialized in feeding on dense concentrations of copepods. The species has been severely depleted by historic commercial and illegal Soviet whaling. It inhabits cold temperate to subarctic waters and has a low reproductive rate with females first calving at 9-10 years. Conservation efforts are challenged by the species' rarity, remote habitat, and threats from human activities including fishing gear entanglement and oil industry impacts.

key_facts: Endemic to Ángel de la Guarda Island and nearby islets in Mexico; confined to sandy beaches and rocky areas; three subspecies recognized, two extinct; no specimens found since 1991; likely extinct or restricted to less than 10 km2; threatened by feral cats and invasive rodents; replaced by house mouse in habitat.
Nocturnal species found only in cloud forests of Sierra de Taxco, Mexico, at elevations 1800-2400m; small rodent with cinnamon to reddish brown fur and white underparts.

key_facts: Hooded seals are large phocids with distinct sexual dimorphism and a unique inflatable nasal bladder in males used for communication and mating. They inhabit drifting pack ice and deep waters in the North Atlantic and Arctic Ocean. They have a very short lactation period of 4 days with high-fat milk, and pups grow rapidly. Populations in the NW Atlantic are stable or increasing due to conservation efforts, while NE Atlantic populations have declined significantly. Major threats include hunting and bycatch. They are protected under the Marine Mammal Protection Act of 1972.
